params.text:
An uncontrolled work environment

You cannot redesign someone's home, and you should not try to. It is their private space, arranged the way they want it. It is also your workplace, and the things in it that could hurt you belong in the care plan and risk assessment rather than being quietly put up with.

  • Pets and dogs: ask for animals to be shut in another room before you start. A dog that is settled with its owner may react very differently to you kneeling beside their chair at seven in the morning.
  • Smoking and oxygen: oxygen makes fabrics, hair and emollient creams burn fiercely. Nobody smokes near cylinders or concentrators, and if it is happening you raise it the same day.
  • Clutter and hoarding: narrow walkways, stacked belongings and blocked exits make moving and handling unsafe and slow any evacuation. Describe what you found rather than squeezing past it every visit.
  • Lighting and stairs: dim bulbs, loose carpet, missing handrails and dark hallways cause falls — yours as well as theirs.
  • Electrics and heating: scorched sockets, trailing leads, extension blocks plugged into each other, heaters pushed against furniture. If you smell gas, leave, ring the national gas emergency line and tell the office.
  • Infection risk: soiled bedding, no hot water or soap, sharps left out. Use your PPE, follow hand hygiene, and never handle a needle without a proper sharps container.

People, substances and things that unsettle you

Physical hazards are easier to name than human ones, but human ones are what most often end a visit early.

  • Other people present: family, friends, neighbours or someone you have never met. You are entitled to ask who they are and why they are there, and to record anyone whose behaviour concerns you.
  • Alcohol and substances: intoxication makes behaviour unpredictable and consent unclear. Keep yourself between the person and the door, avoid arguing, and leave if the mood shifts.
  • Weapons and intimidating items: knives left out, air weapons, replicas, or objects used to unnerve you. This is never something to normalise.
  • Your instinct: if it feels wrong, that is enough. Withdraw, get somewhere safe, then ring the office and explain.

Leaving a call on safety grounds is not abandoning anyone. Tell your manager immediately so the person's care can be covered another way, and write it up while it is fresh. Some incidents are reportable under RIDDOR, and every report you make is what turns a hazard one worker endured into a control the next worker inherits.

params.question: A hazard in a service user's home only needs reporting once it has actually caused an injury.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Waiting for an injury defeats the point. Anything that could hurt you or the person you support — a blocked stairway, smoking near oxygen, an aggressive visitor — goes to your manager and into the record straight away.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Right. Hazards are reported when you spot them, not after someone gets hurt. Near misses and long-standing risks both belong in the record so the risk assessment and care plan can be updated.
